# Source Card: Duffy-Null Phenotype-Associated Neutropenia is the Most Common Etiology for Leukopenia/Neutropenia Referrals to a Tertiary Children's Hospital

## Summary
This peer-reviewed brief report (J Pediatr 2023, Children's Hospital of Michigan, Detroit; PMID 37419240, DOI 10.1016/j.jpeds.2023.113608) establishes that Duffy-null phenotype-associated neutropenia — a benign, non-pathologic cause of low neutrophil counts — is the single most common etiology behind pediatric leukopenia/neutropenia referrals, at 77.7% overall, with subgroup prevalence up to 96.6% (Yemeni), 91% (African American), and 52.9% (non-Yemeni Middle Eastern). For the pediatric CDS module it supports (a) high pretest probability of benign ethnic/Duffy-null neutropenia in ancestrally relevant pediatric populations, and (b) absence of recurrent/frequent/serious infection as the practical discriminator to avoid over-referral. It defines NO absolute neutrophil count (ANC) threshold.

## Population / assay / lifecycle notes
- Population: pediatric leukopenia/neutropenia REFERRALS at one tertiary center (Detroit); ethnically diverse (Yemeni, African American, non-Yemeni Middle Eastern subgroups explicitly reported). Figures are referral-cohort proportions, NOT general-population prevalence and NOT reference intervals.
- Assay/method: percentages are epidemiological proportions of Duffy-null-associated neutropenia, not analyzer-dependent numeric cutoffs; Duffy status ascertained by phenotype/genotype (specific method not confirmed from the abstract).
- Lifecycle: no guideline version, effective date, or supersession — this is primary observational literature, not a versioned standard.

## Scope-exit / referral relevance (if any)
- YES — this source directly informs referral-triage scope. It frames the ABSENCE of recurrent/frequent/serious infection as the signal that neutropenia is likely benign (Duffy-null/ethnic) and does not warrant escalation, and proposes Duffy typing as a de-escalation tool. Note (ev_005) is an implementation_proposal (the authors' recommendation), not an executable rule; it must NOT be encoded as a directive. Presence of recurrent/frequent/serious infection remains the red-flag that keeps a case in scope for further workup.

## Limitations & conflicts
- GAP (numeric): No absolute neutrophil count (ANC) threshold defining "neutropenia" is locatable in this source (abstract carries no ANC cutoff; full text paywalled and not retrieved). Any ANC cutoff for the CDS module must come from a threshold-defining reference, not this paper.
- Denominator/context figures seen in secondary summaries (study window June 1, 2016 – May 31, 2019; leukopenia/neutropenia = 12.6% of hematology referrals) are NOT verbatim-confirmed here and are therefore NOT asserted as evidence points; treat as background only until full text is obtained.
- Single-center, single-metro (Detroit) referral population with a distinctive ethnic mix (notably Yemeni); prevalence figures are cohort- and geography-specific and should not be generalized as population base rates.
- Secondary source_rank: observational brief report; does not establish causation, diagnostic criteria, or management directives.
- No conflicts silently collapsed. Potential comparison point (not a resolved conflict): a separate urban safety-net cohort (Parekh 2025, Advances in Hematology) reports Fy(a-b-)/Duffy-null incidence among neutropenia referrals — magnitudes may differ by setting; preserve as distinct data points rather than averaging.
